content.text: CI/CD Pipeline on Self-Hosted Kubernetes

A **production-grade CI/CD pipeline** built on GitHub Actions, deploying to a self-hosted k3s Kubernetes cluster at `k8s-api.vpjoshi.in`. Every project in the homelab ecosystem — Stats App, S3 Drive, SyncDocs, JoshiOTT, Portfolio, and homelab services — is deployed through the same battle-tested pipeline pattern.

The trigger is intentionally simple — include `[prod]` in a commit message and GitHub Actions takes it from there. Build the Docker image, push to GitHub Container Registry, connect to the self-hosted K8s cluster, apply manifests, and do a rolling restart. Zero manual SSH, zero downtime.

content.title: Live Pipeline
content.text: Stats App GitHub Actions runs are visible at **https://github.com/Joshi-labs/stats/actions** — every `[prod]` deploy is logged there with full build and deploy output.
The Problem

Running a self-hosted Kubernetes cluster is only half the problem. Deploying to it manually — SSH into the server, pull the latest code, rebuild the Docker image, push, update the K8s deployment — is slow, error-prone, and doesn't scale across multiple projects.

Managed CI/CD services like Vercel, Railway, or Render only work with their own infrastructure. For a self-hosted k3s cluster on a home server, you need a pipeline that can reach the K8s API securely from GitHub's runners — without exposing the cluster publicly in an insecure way.

content.title: Core Challenges
content.text: **Secure K8s API access** from GitHub runners · **No accidental deploys** on every push · **Multi-project pipeline** without duplicating workflow files · **Zero downtime** rolling updates on a single-node homelab cluster · **GHCR auth** for private container images
Solution Overview

items: **`[prod]` commit trigger** — only commits containing `[prod]` in the message trigger a deploy. Regular pushes run lint/type-check only. Prevents accidental deploys during active development.
**GHCR (GitHub Container Registry)** — Docker images pushed to `ghcr.io/joshi-labs/<project>:latest`. Free for public repos, authenticated via `GITHUB_TOKEN` — no external registry needed.

**`ci-user` service account** — a dedicated K8s service account with a permanent token scoped to the `apps-prod` namespace. GitHub Actions authenticates to the cluster using this token via a kubeconfig built at runtime.
**k8s-api.vpjoshi.in** — the K8s API server is exposed publicly via Cloudflare Tunnel, so GitHub's runners can reach it without VPN or SSH tunneling.
**Rolling restart** — `kubectl rollout restart` triggers a rolling update. The cluster pulls the new image and replaces pods one at a time — zero downtime even on a single-node setup.
**`kubectl rollout status`** — the pipeline blocks until the rollout completes. If the new pod fails health checks, the pipeline fails and the old pod stays live.

content.text: Pipeline Architecture

content.code:
Developer Machine
       │
       │  git commit -m "feat: update metric card [prod]"
       │  git push origin main
       │
       ▼
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│              GitHub (Joshi-labs org)             │
│                                                 │
│  push event → check commit message             │
│       │                                         │
│       ├── contains [prod]?                      │
│       │       │                                 │
│       │       yes                               │
│       │       ▼                                 │
│  ┌────────────────────────────────────────┐     │
│  │         GitHub Actions Runner          │     │
│  │         (ubuntu-latest)                │     │
│  │                                        │     │
│  │  1. checkout code                      │     │
│  │  2. docker buildx setup                │     │
│  │  3. login to GHCR                      │     │
│  │  4. build + push image                 │     │
│  │     ghcr.io/joshi-labs/<app>:latest    │     │
│  │  5. install kubectl                    │     │
│  │  6. build kubeconfig from secrets      │     │
│  │  7. kubectl apply -f k8s/              │     │
│  │  8. kubectl rollout restart            │     │
│  │  9. kubectl rollout status (block)     │     │
│  └────────────────┬───────────────────────┘     │
│                   │ HTTPS (K8S_TOKEN)            │
└───────────────────┼─────────────────────────────┘
                    │
                    ▼
        k8s-api.vpjoshi.in
        (Cloudflare Tunnel → K8s API)
                    │
                    ▼
┌───────────────────────────────────────┐
│     k3s Cluster (homelab server)      │
│                                       │
│  apps-prod namespace                  │
│  ┌─────────────────────────────────┐  │
│  │  deployment/<app>               │  │
│  │  → pull new image from GHCR     │  │
│  │  → rolling restart (0 downtime) │  │
│  │  → health check passes          │  │
│  │  → old pod terminated           │  │
│  └─────────────────────────────────┘  │
└───────────────────────────────────────┘

content.title: K8s API via Cloudflare Tunnel
content.text: The K8s API server is exposed at `k8s-api.vpjoshi.in` via Cloudflare Tunnel — no open ports on the home router, no VPN needed. GitHub Actions runners reach the cluster over HTTPS using the `ci-user` service account token stored as a GitHub secret.
GitHub Actions Workflow
Every project uses the same core workflow pattern — only the image name, deployment name, and namespace differ. Here's the Stats App workflow in full:

content.code:
# .github/workflows/deploy.yml
name: Build and Deploy Stats App

on:
  push:
    branches:
      - main
      - master

jobs:
  deploy:
    if: contains(github.event.head_commit.message, '[prod]')
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    permissions:
      contents: read
      packages: write
    env:
      IMAGE: ghcr.io/joshi-labs/stats:latest
      NAMESPACE: apps-prod

    steps:
      - name: Checkout code
        uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Set up Docker Buildx
        uses: docker/setup-buildx-action@v3

      - name: Login to GHCR
        uses: docker/login-action@v3
        with:
          registry: ghcr.io
          username: ${{ github.repository_owner }}
          password: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}

      - name: Build and push image
        uses: docker/build-push-action@v5
        with:
          context: .
          push: true
          tags: ${{ env.IMAGE }}

      - name: Install kubectl
        run: |
          KUBECTL_VERSION=$(curl -L -s https://dl.k8s.io/release/stable.txt)
          curl -L -o kubectl https://dl.k8s.io/release/${KUBECTL_VERSION}/bin/linux/amd64/kubectl
          chmod +x kubectl && sudo mv kubectl /usr/local/bin/kubectl

      - name: Configure kubeconfig
        run: |
          mkdir -p ~/.kube
          cat <<EOF > ~/.kube/config
          apiVersion: v1
          kind: Config
          clusters:
          - name: k3s
            cluster:
              server: ${K8S_API_URL}
              insecure-skip-tls-verify: true
          users:
          - name: ci-user
            user:
              token: ${K8S_TOKEN}
          contexts:
          - name: ci
            context:
              cluster: k3s
              user: ci-user
              namespace: ${NAMESPACE}
          current-context: ci
          EOF
        env:
          K8S_API_URL: ${{ secrets.K8S_API_URL }}
          K8S_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.K8S_TOKEN }}

      - name: Deploy to Kubernetes
        run: |
          kubectl apply -f k8s/ --validate=false
          kubectl rollout restart deployment stats-app -n ${NAMESPACE}
          kubectl rollout status deployment stats-app -n ${NAMESPACE}

content.title: Why --validate=false?
content.text: `--validate=false` skips server-side schema validation on `kubectl apply` — necessary because the GitHub runner's kubectl version may differ from the cluster version, causing false validation failures on perfectly valid manifests.
ci-user – K8s Service Account

A dedicated **`ci-user` service account** was created in the `apps-prod` namespace with a **permanent token** and scoped RBAC permissions. It can apply manifests and manage deployments in `apps-prod` only — it cannot touch other namespaces, cluster resources, or secrets.

content.code:
# ci-user service account + permanent token
apiVersion: v1
kind: ServiceAccount
metadata:
  name: ci-user
  namespace: apps-prod
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Secret
metadata:
  name: ci-user-token
  namespace: apps-prod
  annotations:
    kubernetes.io/service-account.name: ci-user
type: kubernetes.io/service-account-token
---
# RBAC — scoped to apps-prod namespace only
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: Role
metadata:
  name: ci-deployer
  namespace: apps-prod
rules:
  - apiGroups: ["apps"]
    resources: ["deployments"]
    verbs: ["get", "list", "apply", "patch", "update"]
  - apiGroups: [""]
    resources: ["services", "configmaps"]
    verbs: ["get", "list", "apply", "patch"]
---
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: RoleBinding
metadata:
  name: ci-deployer-binding
  namespace: apps-prod
subjects:
  - kind: ServiceAccount
    name: ci-user
    namespace: apps-prod
roleRef:
  kind: Role
  name: ci-deployer
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io

content.title: Least Privilege
content.text: The `ci-user` token is stored as a **GitHub Actions secret** (`K8S_TOKEN`). It has no cluster-admin privileges — scoped to `apps-prod` namespace only. Even if the token were compromised, an attacker could only interact with deployments in that one namespace.
Pipeline in Action

content.caption: Stats App — live deploy via GitHub Actions. Code pushed with [prod] → image built → deployed to k3s in under 2 minutes.

content.title: Live Action Runs
content.text: Every Stats App deploy is logged at **https://github.com/Joshi-labs/stats/actions** — full build output, timing, and rollout status for every `[prod]` commit.
Projects Deployed via This Pipeline
Every project in the homelab ecosystem uses the same `[prod]` commit trigger pattern — only the image name, deployment name, and K8s manifest differ. Here's what's running through the pipeline:
Stats App (stats.vpjoshi.in)

Next.js 15 public monitoring dashboard querying Prometheus. Deployed to `apps-prod` namespace. The reference implementation for the pipeline — the workflow above is exactly what runs for this project.

content.code:
IMAGE:      ghcr.io/joshi-labs/stats:latest
DEPLOYMENT: stats-app
NAMESPACE:  apps-prod
TRIGGER:    [prod] commit on main

content.text: S3 Drive (s3-drive.vpjoshi.in)

Self-hosted Google Drive alternative — Go binary with embedded React frontend. Single pod deployment. The Go binary + React frontend build happens inside the Docker build, so the GitHub runner just needs to run `docker build`.

content.code:
IMAGE:      ghcr.io/joshi-labs/s3drive:latest
DEPLOYMENT: s3drive
NAMESPACE:  apps-prod
BUILD:      npm run build inside Dockerfile → go build (embeds frontend)

content.text: SyncDocs (sync-docs.vpjoshi.in)

Real-time collaborative document editor on AWS EKS — the pipeline here targets AWS EKS instead of the homelab k3s cluster. Uses the same `[prod]` trigger but `kubectl` is configured against the EKS endpoint.

content.code:
IMAGE:      ghcr.io/joshi-labs/syncdocs:latest
DEPLOYMENT: syncdocs-backend
CLUSTER:    AWS EKS (ap-south-1)
TRIGGER:    [prod] commit on main

content.text: JoshiOTT (ott.vpjoshi.in)
Netflix-style OTT streaming platform. Docker Compose on AWS EC2 — the pipeline SSHes into the EC2 instance, pulls the new image, and runs `docker compose up -d` instead of kubectl.

content.code:
IMAGE:      ghcr.io/joshi-labs/joshiott:latest
DEPLOYMENT: docker compose on EC2
TRIGGER:    [prod] commit on main

content.text: Portfolio (vpjoshi.in)
Node.js + React portfolio site running as a Docker container on the homelab. Deployed to k3s via the same pipeline pattern as Stats App.

content.code:
IMAGE:      ghcr.io/joshi-labs/portfolio:latest
DEPLOYMENT: portfolio
NAMESPACE:  apps-prod
TRIGGER:    [prod] commit on main

content.text: Homelab Services (n8n, PocketBase)

Core homelab services — n8n workflow automation and PocketBase BaaS — are also deployed via GitHub Actions. Config changes (env vars, K8s manifests) are committed and deployed with `[prod]` rather than manual `kubectl apply` on the server.

content.code:
# n8n
IMAGE:      n8nio/n8n:latest (upstream, no custom build)
DEPLOYMENT: n8n
NAMESPACE:  apps-prod

# PocketBase
IMAGE:      ghcr.io/joshi-labs/pocketbase:latest
DEPLOYMENT: pocketbase
NAMESPACE:  apps-prod

content.text: The [prod] Commit Trigger Pattern

All pipelines share the same trigger logic — a commit message check before any expensive steps run. Regular pushes (bug fixes, WIP commits, README updates) don't trigger a deploy. Only intentional `[prod]` commits hit the cluster.

content.code:
# Regular push — no deploy
git commit -m "fix: typo in dashboard label"
git push
# → GitHub Actions skips deploy job entirely

# Production deploy
git commit -m "feat: add network drops metric card [prod]"
git push
# → GitHub Actions: build → push → deploy → rollout status

# The trigger condition in every workflow
jobs:
  deploy:
    if: contains(github.event.head_commit.message, '[prod]')

content.title: Why Not Branch-Based Triggers?
content.text: Branch-based triggers (e.g. deploy on push to `main`) cause accidental deploys during active development. The `[prod]` pattern gives **explicit intent** — you decide per-commit whether it goes to production. No separate `release` branch needed, no PR merge ceremony.
Zero Downtime Rolling Updates

K8s rolling updates replace pods one at a time. On a single-node homelab this still works — the new pod starts, passes readiness checks, then the old pod is terminated. The pipeline blocks on `kubectl rollout status` so a failed deploy is caught immediately.

content.code:
# What happens during a rolling restart

kubectl rollout restart deployment stats-app -n apps-prod
# → K8s creates new pod with new image
# → New pod pulls ghcr.io/joshi-labs/stats:latest
# → Readiness probe passes (HTTP 200 on /)
# → Old pod receives SIGTERM, graceful shutdown
# → Old pod terminated

kubectl rollout status deployment stats-app -n apps-prod
# → Waiting for deployment "stats-app" rollout to finish...
# → deployment "stats-app" successfully rolled out
# → Pipeline step exits 0 ✓

# If new pod fails:
# → Readiness probe fails repeatedly
# → K8s does NOT terminate old pod
# → rollout status exits non-zero
# → Pipeline fails, old pod stays live ✓

content.title: Automatic Rollback Safety
content.text: If the new image crashes on startup or fails readiness checks, `kubectl rollout status` returns a non-zero exit code — failing the pipeline. The old pod is **never terminated** until the new one is healthy. On a homelab with no redundant nodes, this is the safety net that prevents outages from bad deploys.
Secrets & Security
Three GitHub Actions secrets power the entire pipeline across all projects:

content.code:
# GitHub Actions Secrets (repo or org level)
K8S_API_URL   → https://k8s-api.vpjoshi.in   (K8s API endpoint)
K8S_TOKEN     → <ci-user permanent token>     (scoped to apps-prod)
GITHUB_TOKEN  → auto-provided by GitHub       (GHCR push auth)

items: **`K8S_API_URL`** – The K8s API server URL. Public via Cloudflare Tunnel but requires a valid token to authenticate.
**`K8S_TOKEN`** – The `ci-user` permanent service account token. Scoped to `apps-prod` namespace only — no cluster-admin.
**`GITHUB_TOKEN`** – Automatically provided by GitHub Actions. Used to push Docker images to GHCR. No manual secret needed.

content.title: insecure-skip-tls-verify
content.text: `insecure-skip-tls-verify: true` is set in the kubeconfig because the k3s API server uses a self-signed certificate. The connection is still encrypted — TLS is active, only certificate verification is skipped. For production multi-team clusters, the CA cert should be passed explicitly instead.
Tech Stack

items: **GHCR (GitHub Container Registry)** – Private container image registry, free for the Joshi-labs org.
**kubectl** – Installed fresh on each runner, latest stable version.
**k3s** – Self-hosted lightweight Kubernetes cluster on the homelab server.
**Cloudflare Tunnel** – Exposes `k8s-api.vpjoshi.in` securely without open ports.
**K8s RBAC** – `ci-user` service account scoped to `apps-prod` namespace.
**Kubernetes Rolling Updates** – Zero downtime deploys with automatic rollback on failure.
